title: Structured Reasoning With Evidence
description: Design a prompt that receives a customer support incident timeline and makes the model analyze the likely root cause without guessing. The response should separate evidence, reasoning, and conclusion clearly: include quoted evidence from the timeline, a reasoning section that explains how the evidence supports the conclusion, and a final answer with the most likely root cause, confidence level, and next action.
input_format: The input is a chronological incident log with timestamps, status changes, and support notes.
evaluator_expectation: The response must include XML sections for quoted evidence, reasoning, and final answer. The conclusion should identify the most likely cause, confidence, and next action. Quotes must reference actual input data.
input_wrapper_template:
<context>
You are solving an interview-style debugging prompt.
</context>
<input>
{input}
</input>

sample_cases.name: API timeout incident
sample_cases.input_data:
09:00 deploy started
09:07 deploy completed
09:12 alerts fired for checkout latency
09:14 support note: only EU region impacted
09:18 infra note: database CPU normal
09:20 infra note: cache miss rate spiked from 3% to 48%
09:24 rollback completed
09:27 latency recovered
sample_cases.expected_output:
<quotes>
  <quote>09:20 infra note: cache miss rate spiked from 3% to 48%</quote>
</quotes>
<reasoning>
  ...
</reasoning>
<final_answer>
  <root_cause>Cache configuration regression after deploy</root_cause>
  <confidence>high</confidence>
  <next_action>Inspect deployment diff for cache invalidation or routing changes</next_action>
</final_answer>

sample_cases.name: Database connection pool exhaustion
sample_cases.input_data:
10:00 monitoring: all services healthy, DB pool utilization at 22%
10:15 deploy: analytics-worker v3.12 rolled out to production (adds new hourly aggregation job)
10:18 monitoring: DB pool utilization climbed to 41%
10:22 monitoring: DB pool utilization at 67%, query latency p99 up from 45ms to 320ms
10:25 support ticket: customer reports slow page loads on the reporting tab
10:27 monitoring: DB pool utilization at 94%, active connections 189/200
10:28 infra note: checked DB CPU — only at 35%, disk I/O normal
10:30 infra note: slow query log shows hundreds of identical SELECT on events table with no LIMIT clause
10:31 infra note: all slow queries originate from analytics-worker service
10:34 developer note: v3.12 changelog shows new aggregation job opens a connection per partition (96 partitions)
10:38 rollback: analytics-worker reverted to v3.11
10:41 monitoring: DB pool utilization dropped to 28%, query latency p99 back to 50ms
10:43 support note: customer confirmed reporting tab is responsive again
sample_cases.expected_output:
<quotes>
  <quote>10:15 deploy: analytics-worker v3.12 rolled out to production (adds new hourly aggregation job)</quote>
  <quote>10:30 infra note: slow query log shows hundreds of identical SELECT on events table with no LIMIT clause</quote>
  <quote>10:34 developer note: v3.12 changelog shows new aggregation job opens a connection per partition (96 partitions)</quote>
</quotes>
<reasoning>
The timeline shows a clear correlation between the analytics-worker v3.12 deploy at 10:15 and the steady rise in connection pool utilization. DB CPU and disk I/O were normal, ruling out a compute or storage bottleneck. The slow query log confirms the source: hundreds of identical unbounded SELECT queries from the analytics-worker. The v3.12 changelog reveals the new aggregation job opens one connection per partition (96 partitions), which nearly exhausted the 200-connection pool. Rollback to v3.11 immediately resolved the issue.
</reasoning>
<final_answer>
  <root_cause>New hourly aggregation job in analytics-worker v3.12 opens one DB connection per partition, exhausting the connection pool</root_cause>
  <confidence>high</confidence>
  <next_action>Fix the aggregation job to use a shared connection pool or batch partitions, then re-deploy with connection limit safeguards</next_action>
</final_answer>
sample_cases.notes: Tests multi-evidence reasoning with deploy correlation and resource exhaustion pattern.

sample_cases.name: Gradual memory leak causing service restarts
sample_cases.input_data:
Monday 06:00 deploy: notification-service v2.8.0 deployed (added rich-text email rendering)
Monday 08:00 monitoring: heap usage 1.2GB (normal baseline is 1.0-1.4GB)
Monday 14:00 monitoring: heap usage 2.1GB
Monday 22:00 monitoring: heap usage 3.4GB, GC pause times increasing
Tuesday 03:17 incident: notification-service OOM killed, auto-restarted by orchestrator
Tuesday 03:19 monitoring: heap usage back to 1.1GB after restart
Tuesday 06:00 infra note: reviewed metrics — heap grows ~120MB/hour steadily since Monday deploy
Tuesday 09:00 monitoring: heap usage at 1.8GB again (6 hours after restart)
Tuesday 10:30 developer note: profiler shows objects accumulating in RichTextRenderer.templateCache — cache has no eviction policy
Tuesday 10:45 developer note: each unique email template variant creates a new compiled entry, and the marketing team launched a campaign Monday morning with 47 personalized subject-line variants
Tuesday 11:00 developer note: v2.7.x used plain-text only, had no template cache
Tuesday 12:00 hotfix: deployed v2.8.1 with LRU eviction (max 500 entries) on template cache
Tuesday 13:00 monitoring: heap stable at 1.3GB after 1 hour
sample_cases.expected_output:
<quotes>
  <quote>Tuesday 10:30 developer note: profiler shows objects accumulating in RichTextRenderer.templateCache — cache has no eviction policy</quote>
  <quote>Tuesday 10:45 developer note: each unique email template variant creates a new compiled entry, and the marketing team launched a campaign Monday morning with 47 personalized subject-line variants</quote>
  <quote>Tuesday 11:00 developer note: v2.7.x used plain-text only, had no template cache</quote>
</quotes>
<reasoning>
The heap grew linearly at ~120MB/hour starting immediately after the v2.8.0 deploy on Monday. The profiler pinpointed the leak to RichTextRenderer.templateCache, which was introduced in v2.8.0 (v2.7.x had no such cache). The cache compiled and stored a new entry for every unique template variant without eviction. The marketing campaign with 47 personalized variants accelerated accumulation. The OOM kill on Tuesday and the subsequent repeat growth pattern confirm the leak is deterministic, not load-dependent. The hotfix adding LRU eviction stabilized heap usage.
</reasoning>
<final_answer>
  <root_cause>Unbounded template cache in RichTextRenderer introduced in v2.8.0 — every unique email variant created a permanent cache entry with no eviction</root_cause>
  <confidence>high</confidence>
  <next_action>Verify the LRU-evicted hotfix holds under sustained campaign volume, and add a heap-growth alerting threshold to catch similar leaks earlier</next_action>
</final_answer>
sample_cases.notes: Tests reasoning across a multi-day timeline with gradual degradation and profiler evidence.

hidden_cases.name: Billing queue slowdown
hidden_cases.input_data:
11:00 queue depth 20
11:08 queue depth 450
11:10 note: background worker release finished at 11:05
11:11 note: no DB saturation
11:13 note: retries doubled after release
11:25 rollback worker release
11:31 queue depth back to 22

hidden_cases.name: Login failures spike
hidden_cases.input_data:
14:00 auth service healthy
14:12 CDN cert rotation completed
14:15 error rate jumped from 0.1% to 12%
14:16 support note: users see SSL errors on login page
14:18 infra note: new cert has wrong SAN entries
14:22 cert re-issued with correct SANs
14:25 error rate back to 0.2%
hidden_cases.notes: Tests whether reasoning correctly links cert rotation to SSL errors.

config.failure_message: Confidence must be one of: high, medium, low.
The reasoning section should contain a meaningful analysis (at least a few sentences).
